text: Surgery of the pancreas remains one of the most talented fields in general surgery. There is only scarce data on robotic approach of pancreatic lesions. Tumors of pancreatic tail can be resected laparoscopically with good results. Robotics may offer a more stable platform for dissection and mobilization of such tumors, especially when the spleen should be preserved. Whipple pancreatodermatoma is a long and challenging procedure which may be facilitated from robotics.

We present here our experience with robotic pancreatic cases including six patients that underwent distal pancreatectomy as in the video already showing and two of them with spleen preservation. In one case There was a large pancreatic cyst removal and two cases that underwent the WIPI pancretodermectomy using a totally robotic technique.

Based on an extensive experience with paroscoping and robotic operations and after a series of successful digital pancreatectomies and hybrid pancretodermectomies, we established a totally robotic technique for WIPI pancretodermectomy.

The surgeon performs advanced dissection near the splenic hilum and above the pancreas, identifying major splenic trunks. Da Vinci robotic instruments are used for precise dissection around the vessels, employing vessel loops to control each vessel separately, minimizing the risk of trauma and bleeding, particularly important for spleen preservation.

The surgeon has dissected the pancreatic tail free from surrounding tissues, creating a plane to push it over the vessels. The final detachment of the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum is visible, followed by division of the tail using a linear stapler, including the mass within it.
This mass eventually was found to be a benign ectopic splenic mass. The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged a few days after surgery without any complications.
The surgeon removes the specimen using an ento bag, followed by the extension of the Kocher maneuver, which involves lifting the duodenum to improve exposure.

The surgeon uses the SI system to maneuver to the right abdomen, focusing on the root of the mesentery. The fore portion of the duodenum and the duodenal jejunal junction are visible, with the jejunum being freed. The Whipple specimen is nearly prepared for removal.

For the first patient, the total operative time was approximately nine hours. Patient received clear liquids on postoperative day four and was discharged on day ten. Histology showed a ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head with six of fifteen peripancreatic lymph nodes involved. The stage was T-III-N-I.

For the second patient, total operative time was approximately seven hours. The patient received clear liquids on day six and was discharged on day twelve. Histology revealed a ductal adenocarcinoma and invasion of one out of ten regional lymph nodes. The stage was T-III-N-I. In both patients, the robotic platform proved useful during dissection near the SMV and during construction of the anastomosis.

Robotic assistance in pancreatic surgery may overcome the limitations of laparoscopy with potentially lower blood loss and morbidity. Of course, further randomized trials and longer follow-up will be necessary to validate these results.

The surgeon divides the common bile duct and locates the stent, followed by its removal. The cholecystectomy is completed, and the specimen is removed through a small extension of the right upper robotic incision. Pneumoperitoneum is re-established as the incision is sutured, and the robotic system is redocked. The continuity of the gastrointestinal tract is repaired using threonostomosis.

The surgeon completes the pancreatic gastric anastomosis and the duodenal jejunal anastomosis using a linear stapler, followed by suturing the defect with robotic needle holders. The creation of the biliojejunal anastomosis is also performed, showcasing the precision of robotic suturing in narrow spaces.
The robotic hook is used to carefully manipulate tissues, showcasing the final result of the pancreaticoduodenectomy operation.
Thank you for your attention. Although our presentation includes use of the DaVinci SI system, we have no disclosures to make. Thank you very much.
